move.description: The event is part of the main narrative action: it drives the story forward through conflict, preparation, or external help. Choose this if the event involves the hero acting, reacting, or being acted upon within the core plot.

setup.description: The event describes the world or the hero before any conflict or disruption has occurred. No threat, villain, or lack is present yet.

initial_situation.description: The event presents the hero's ordinary life: their home, family, social role, or daily routine. Nothing has gone wrong yet. This is the baseline state before the story begins.

conflict.description: The event introduces or develops a problem: a threat, obstacle, loss, or antagonist action that disrupts the hero's world.

hero_interdiction.description: The event describes a rule, prohibition, or restriction imposed on the hero that limits their freedom of action.

villainy.description: The event describes a harmful act by an antagonist directed at the hero or someone close to them.

false_matrimony.description: The antagonist uses a false promise of marriage or a romantic deception to harm the hero or their family.

expulsion.description: The hero is banished, cast out, or forced to leave their home or community by the antagonist or an unjust authority.

kidnapping.description: A person important to the hero is abducted or taken away by the antagonist.

murder.description: Someone close to the hero is killed by the antagonist, raising the stakes of the conflict.

lack.description: The event describes the hero or their world as missing something essential that motivates action.

lack_of_bride.description: The hero lacks a partner or companion needed to fulfill their personal or social role.

lack_of_money.description: The hero lacks material resources needed to act or survive.

hero_departure.description: The hero leaves their familiar environment to begin the journey or mission, whether by choice or by force.

struggle.description: The hero is in direct confrontation with an antagonist or their agent.

fight.description: The hero engages in physical combat or a direct duel with the villain or their allies.

branding.description: The hero receives a physical mark as a result of conflict — either a lasting sign or a temporary injury.

receive_mark.description: The hero gains a permanent identifying mark (scar, symbol, brand) that will later serve as proof of identity or fate.

receive_injury.description: The hero suffers a physical wound during a confrontation. The injury is temporary and may affect later events.

connective_incident.description: The event links two narrative segments without being a conflict or resolution in itself. It moves the plot forward mechanically.

call_for_help.description: A character asks for assistance, triggering action from the hero or an ally.

departure_decision.description: The hero consciously decides to leave and begin their mission. The decision itself is the event, not the departure.

villain_gains_information.description: The antagonist learns something about the hero — location, weakness, plan — that increases the threat to the hero.

preparation.description: The event shows the hero acquiring what they need — objects, knowledge, allies, or strategy — before facing the main antagonist.

absentation.description: A key character temporarily leaves their environment, creating a gap that enables or triggers later conflict.

breaking_interdiction.description: The hero or another character violates a previously established prohibition, triggering consequences.

acquisition.description: The hero obtains an object, ally, or piece of knowledge that will be needed later.

get_present.description: A donor gives the hero a magical object, tool, or key information, often after the hero passes a test of character.

guidance.description: A mentor, oracle, or sign tells the hero where to go, what to do, or how to defeat the villain.

return.description: The hero returns to a previously visited place during the preparation phase to regroup, collect allies, or complete a task.

make_contact_with_enemy.description: The hero makes direct contact with the antagonist — through disguise, negotiation, or surveillance — before the final confrontation.

mediation.description: A third party intervenes to help the hero move to the next stage of the story.

trickery.description: The hero or villain uses deception, disguise, or manipulation to overcome an obstacle without direct confrontation.

beginning_of_counteraction.description: The hero stops reacting and takes the first deliberate initiative against the source of conflict. This is the turning point from passive to active.

helper_move.description: An ally, magical force, or favorable circumstance directly supports the hero at a critical moment.

receipt_object.description: The hero receives or activates a magical or strategic object at the moment it is needed to overcome a specific obstacle.

liquidiation_of_lack.description: The original lack or deficiency that motivated the hero's quest is resolved.

release_from_captivity.description: A captive character — the hero or someone else — is freed from the antagonist's control.

pursuit_and_rescue.description: The antagonist pursues the hero or escapees, and the hero or allies use evasion or rescue maneuvers to escape.

false_hero_make_unfounded_claim.description: An impostor claims credit for the hero's deeds, creating a false narrative that must be corrected.

attempt_at_reconnaissance.description: The hero or villain tries to gather information about the other side through scouting, interrogation, or magical means.

resolution.description: The event closes or resolves an open conflict. The hero's success is confirmed, the villain is dealt with, and the world reaches a new stable state.

victory.description: The hero definitively overcomes the main antagonist or the central threat.

villain_defeated.description: The antagonist is permanently neutralized: killed, imprisoned, exiled, or stripped of power.

arrival.description: The hero returns to the known world, sometimes without being immediately recognized.

unrecognised_arrival.description: The hero returns but is not yet identified — setting up the recognition episode and exposing any false hero.

home_arrival.description: The hero physically arrives at their home or origin point, completing the journey.

difficult_task_with_solution.description: The community poses an impossible challenge to determine who is the true hero, and it is resolved.

difficult_task.description: An extraordinary test is set that only the true hero can pass.

solution_difficult_task.description: The hero solves the impossible task, publicly proving their identity and merit.

resolution_function.description: Justice is formally established: the hero is acknowledged, the villain punished, and merit rewarded.

recognition.description: The hero's true identity and deeds are publicly acknowledged by the community or authority.

punishment.description: The villain or false hero receives a sanction proportional to their wrongdoing.

reward.description: The hero receives a tangible prize — wealth, title, love — as the social expression of recognition.

exposure_of_villain.description: The antagonist's true identity or guilt is revealed to all characters in the story.

transfiguration.description: The hero undergoes a transformation that marks the end of their arc.

physical_transformation.description: The hero's body changes visibly — rejuvenation, beautification, restoration — as a symbol of their completed journey.

psychological_transformation.description: The hero gains maturity, wisdom, or a fundamental change in character as a result of their journey.

wedding_or_throne.description: The story closes with the hero entering a new social or political status.

wedding.description: The hero's marriage seals the narrative resolution and symbolizes union and a new beginning.

get_throne.description: The hero ascends to a position of power or leadership, confirming their new status.
